questions.question: Explain why digital systems are preferred over analog systems in modern electronic devices.
keyPoints: Digital signals can be regenerated without noise accumulation
Digital systems offer better precision and accuracy
Easier to store, process, and duplicate digital information
Better immunity to noise and interference

questions.question: What is Boolean Algebra and why is it fundamental to digital design?
keyPoints: Mathematical system using binary variables (0,1)
Operations: AND, OR, NOT form basis of all logic circuits
Provides method to analyze and simplify digital circuits
Enables systematic design and optimization of logic functions

questions.question: Define the following terms: (i) Bit (ii) Logic Gate (iii) Integrated Circuit
keyPoints: Bit: Binary digit, smallest data unit (0 or 1)
Logic Gate: Basic circuit performing logical operation on inputs
Integrated Circuit: Electronic circuit with components on single semiconductor chip

questions.question: List and briefly explain three advantages of digital systems.
keyPoints: Noise immunity: Signals can be regenerated maintaining integrity
Storage capability: Digital data easily stored in memory devices
Flexibility and programmability: Same hardware can perform different functions
Reproducibility: Easy to replicate circuits with consistent behavior

questions.question: What is the difference between combinational and sequential circuits? Provide one example of each.
keyPoints: Combinational: Output depends ONLY on current inputs (no memory)
Sequential: Output depends on current inputs AND previous states (has memory)
Examples: Adders, multiplexers are combinational; flip-flops, counters are sequential

questions.question: Explain the concept of logic levels in digital circuits with reference to TTL standards.
keyPoints: Logic levels represent voltage ranges for binary values
TTL standard: LOGIC 0 = 0V to 0.4V, LOGIC 1 = 2.5V to 5V
Voltage ranges provide noise margin for reliable operation
Different families (CMOS, TTL) have different voltage specifications

questions.question: Describe the typical digital design workflow from specification to testing.
keyPoints: 1) Specification: Define requirements
2) Functional Design: High-level behavior
3) Logic Design: Boolean functions and truth tables
4) Circuit Design: Transistor-level implementation
5) Physical Layout: Chip design
6) Fabrication: Manufacturing
7) Testing: Verification

questions.question: Discuss the evolution and importance of digital design in modern computing. Include references to key milestones and technologies.
keyPoints: Started with mechanical computers (1930s-1940s)
真空管 transistors revolutionized electronics
集成电路 (Integrated Circuits) enabled miniaturization
VLSI technology allows billions of transistors on single chip
Hardware Description Languages (Verilog, VHDL) modernized design process
Digital design enables: smartphones, computers, internet, AI - all modern technology

questions.question: Explain Hardware Description Languages (HDLs) and their role in digital design. How do they differ from traditional programming languages?
keyPoints: HDL describes hardware structure and behavior
Parallel execution: all statements execute simultaneously unlike sequential programming
Two main languages: Verilog and VHDL
Enables simulation before fabrication
Synthesis converts HDL to actual hardware gates
Hardware is inherently parallel, software is sequential
Design verification through simulation saves costly fabrication cycles

questions.question: A digital system processes binary data. (a) How many bits are needed to represent 64 different values? (b) How many distinct patterns can be represented using 8 bits? (c) Explain why binary number system is fundamental to digital design.
keyPoints: (a) 6 bits needed (2^6 = 64)
(b) 8 bits can represent 256 patterns (2^8)
(c) Binary maps directly to on/off transistor states
Simple to implement with electronic circuits (high/low voltage)
Boolean algebra provides mathematical foundation
Reliable signal transmission and storage

questions.question: Compare and contrast the following: (i) Digital and Analog systems (ii) Sequential and Combinational circuits (iii) NAND and NOR as universal gates
keyPoints: Digital: discrete values (0/1); Analog: continuous signals
Combinational: output = f(inputs); Sequential: output = f(inputs, state)
NAND and NOR are universal: can implement any Boolean function alone
Both NAND and NOR can create NOT, AND, OR, XOR functions
Universal gates simplify manufacturing and reduce inventory
